MCML analysis of coded discussion interactions

#install latest
#devtools::install_github("mohsaqr/Nestimate")
#devtools::install_github("sonsoleslp/cograph")


library(tidyverse)
library(cograph)
library(Nestimate)
library(tna)
# cograph is referenced via `cograph::splot()` only, not attached, because
# cograph also exports `build_mcml`, `as_tna`, `cluster_summary`, and
# `centrality` and would mask the Nestimate versions used in this analysis.

This document walks through a Multi-Cluster Multi-Level (MCML) analysis on a coded discussion-forum dataset. Each event is a coded utterance (From and To columns). The 26 codes are grouped into six theoretical clusters (Cognitive, Procedural, Question, Reasoning, Social, Tutor) by an external code-mapping table. The pipeline is:

  1. Load the raw event log and the code-mapping table.
  2. Build the MCML in one call, passing the actor / time / action arguments for session detection and the labels argument so within-cluster nodes carry human-readable labels (e.g. Argument instead of c_arg).
  3. Convert to a netobject_group via as_tna() for downstream analysis.
  4. Bootstrap the macro (cluster-level) network for edge stability.
  5. Bootstrap one within-cluster network in detail.
  6. Compute centralities at both levels.
  7. Visualise with cograph::splot().

1. Load the data

The event log has one row per coded message; From is the speaker’s coded utterance and course_group identifies the actor (a course-group thread). The code-mapping table links each code (name) to a cluster (groups) and to a human-readable label (Code).

Interactions <- rio::import(
  "/Users/mohammedsaqr/Downloads/mcml_code/Mcdata/complicated_nw2.csv"
)
Nodesin <- rio::import(
  "/Users/mohammedsaqr/Downloads/mcml_code/Mcdata/Codemapping.xlsx"
)

clusters_list <- split(Nodesin$name, Nodesin$groups)
labels_df     <- Nodesin |> select(name, Code)

length(clusters_list)         # number of clusters
## [1] 6
sapply(clusters_list, length) # nodes per cluster
##  Cognitive Procedural   Question  Reasoning     Social      Tutor 
##          4          3          4          7          4          4

2. Build the MCML

build_mcml() accepts long-format event-log data directly and pipes it through prepare() for session detection. We pass the labels argument so within-cluster nodes carry the human-readable labels from Codemapping. type = "tna" requests row-stochastic transition probabilities at every level (the within-cluster networks are then conditional Markov chains P(next = j | current = i, both i, j ∈ cluster k)).

MCMLL <- Nestimate::build_mcml(
  Interactions,
  clusters = clusters_list,
  actor    = "course_group",
  time     = "timestamp",
  action   = "From",
  type     = "tna",
  labels   = labels_df
)
## Metadata aggregated per session: ties resolved by first occurrence in 'To' (1510 sessions), 'Source' (725 sessions), 'Target' (579 sessions), 'Source_student' (99 sessions), 'Target_Student' (225 sessions), 'CodesFrom' (204 sessions), 'CodesTo' (199 sessions), 'messageID' (421 sessions), 'description.x' (1424 sessions), 'phase_based_on_zimmerman_jarvela_et_al_2016_would_not_maybe_use_these_in_the_analysis_but_included_them_here_anyway_might_be_useful_in_the_paper_when_relating_these_to_the_regulation_framework.x' (194 sessions), 'interaction_type_jarvela_et_al_2016.x' (202 sessions), 'knowledge_construction_socio_emotional_interaction_regulation_tormanen_et_al_2022_zabolotna_et_al_2022.x' (630 sessions), 'examples_zabolotna_et_al_2022.x' (788 sessions), 'references.x' (212 sessions), 'description.y' (1510 sessions), 'phase_based_on_zimmerman_jarvela_et_al_2016_would_not_maybe_use_these_in_the_analysis_but_included_them_here_anyway_might_be_useful_in_the_paper_when_relating_these_to_the_regulation_framework.y' (144 sessions), 'interaction_type_jarvela_et_al_2016.y' (156 sessions), 'knowledge_construction_socio_emotional_interaction_regulation_tormanen_et_al_2022_zabolotna_et_al_2022.y' (509 sessions), 'examples_zabolotna_et_al_2022.y' (652 sessions), 'references.y' (174 sessions)
MCMLL
## MCML Network
## ============
## Type: tna  | Method: sum 
## Nodes: 26  | Clusters: 6 
## Transitions: 21964 
##   Macro: 12370  | Per-cluster: 9594 
## 
## Clusters:
##   Cognitive (4): Argument, Counter, Disagreement, Evaluation
##   Procedural (3): Problem, Management, Team
##   Question (4): Open.Q, Alt.Q, Verification, Critical.Q
##   Reasoning (7): Argument, Classification, Comparison, Conclusion, Statement, Acceptance, Objectives
##   Social (4): Experience, Feelings, Socializing, Materials
##   Tutor (4): Encouraging, Feedback, Guiding, Socializing
## 
## Macro (cluster-level) weights:
##            Cognitive Procedural Question Reasoning Social  Tutor
## Cognitive     0.3016     0.0059   0.0443    0.2299 0.3991 0.0192
## Procedural    0.0152     0.5005   0.0087    0.3369 0.1246 0.0141
## Question      0.1457     0.0110   0.2629    0.2180 0.3165 0.0460
## Reasoning     0.1012     0.0495   0.0342    0.2989 0.5038 0.0125
## Social        0.1275     0.0102   0.0281    0.3089 0.5216 0.0037
## Tutor         0.0418     0.0065   0.0223    0.0526 0.0216 0.8552

5. Convert to a netobject_group

as_tna() wraps every layer (macro plus one per cluster) as a netobject suitable for the rest of the package’s workflow (bootstrap, centrality, cograph visualisation). Initial probabilities propagate through.

MCMLL_tna <- Nestimate::as_tna(MCMLL)
# One figure per layer (macro + each cluster), instead of all stacked
# in a single combined plot.
lapply(MCMLL_tna, splot)

## $macro
## Cograph network: 6 nodes, 35 edges ( directed )
## Source: matrix 
##   Nodes (6): Cognitive, Procedural, Question, Reasoning, Social, Tutor
##   Edges: 29 / 30 (density: 96.7%)
##   Weights: [0.010, 0.500]  |  mean: 0.112
##   Strongest edges:
##     Reasoning -> Social  0.500
##     Cognitive -> Social  0.400
##     Procedural -> Reasoning  0.340
##     Question -> Social  0.320
##     Social -> Reasoning  0.310
##   Self-loops: 6  |  range: [0.260, 0.860]
## Layout: set 
## 
## $Cognitive
## Cograph network: 4 nodes, 15 edges ( directed )
## Source: matrix 
##   Nodes (4): Argument, Counter, Disagreement, Evaluation
##   Edges: 11 / 12 (density: 91.7%)
##   Weights: [0.010, 0.520]  |  mean: 0.139
##   Strongest edges:
##     Argument -> Evaluation  0.520
##     Evaluation -> Argument  0.500
##     Disagreement -> Argument  0.200
##     Disagreement -> Evaluation  0.130
##     Disagreement -> Counter  0.070
##   Self-loops: 4  |  range: [0.460, 0.930]
## Layout: set 
##

##     Feedback -> Guiding  0.230
##     Encouraging -> Socializing  0.170
##   Self-loops: 4  |  range: [0.160, 0.490]
## Layout: set

6. Bootstrap the macro network

bootstrap_network() resamples the recoded sequences and recomputes the transition matrix on every iteration, returning per-edge means, 95% CIs, and a CS-style stability flag (Epskamp, Borsboom & Fried, 2018).

set.seed(2026)
boot_macro <- bootstrap_network(MCMLL, iter = 1000)
boot_macro
## Grouped Bootstrap  [7 groups | 1000 iterations | 95% CI]
##   macro                 15 sig / 30 total
##   Cognitive             2 sig / 11 total
##   Procedural            2 sig / 6 total
##   Question              0 sig / 11 total
##   Reasoning             4 sig / 21 total
##   Social                6 sig / 11 total
##   Tutor                 4 sig / 12 total
# MCMLL$clusters$Cognitive$data

The print summary tells you how many cluster-to-cluster edges survived the stability criterion at the chosen confidence level. The forest plot shows each edge’s mean weight with the bootstrap CI.

7. Bootstrap a within-cluster network

The Cognitive cluster has four sub-codes. Bootstrapping at this level tests which within-cluster transitions are stable.

set.seed(2026)
boot_cog <- bootstrap_network(MCMLL, iter = 500)
boot_cog
## Grouped Bootstrap  [7 groups | 500 iterations | 95% CI]
##   macro                 15 sig / 30 total
##   Cognitive             2 sig / 11 total
##   Procedural            2 sig / 6 total
##   Question              0 sig / 11 total
##   Reasoning             4 sig / 21 total
##   Social                6 sig / 11 total
##   Tutor                 4 sig / 12 total
# A within-cluster bootstrap may have few or no edges surviving the default
# stability criterion. The forest plot only renders if there is at least one
# significant edge, so we guard the call.
if (isTRUE(any(boot_cog$significant))) {
  cograph::plot_bootstrap_forest(boot_cog)
} else {
  message("No within-cluster edges met the default stability criterion ",
          "for the Cognitive cluster — forest plot skipped.")
}
## No within-cluster edges met the default stability criterion for the Cognitive cluster — forest plot skipped.

8. Centralities

cograph::centrality() computes the standard set of network centrality measures (prestige, brokerage, etc.) on a netobject. We run it on the macro and on each within-cluster netobject.

cograph::centrality(MCMLL_tna$macro)
##         node degree_all strength_all closeness_all betweenness eigenvector
## 1  Cognitive         10    1.1297814      13.58042           0  0.38304758
## 2 Procedural         10    0.5824564      19.93632          11  0.08821032
## 3   Question         10    0.8746967      11.78838           0  0.12357648
## 4  Reasoning         10    1.8473701      10.00309           0  0.77022736
## 5     Social         10    1.8440367      15.37584           0  1.00000000
## 6      Tutor         10    0.2403084      19.93592          11  0.04662905
##     pagerank
## 1 0.16616978
## 2 0.05425294
## 3 0.07072488
## 4 0.32252205
## 5 0.34523720
## 6 0.04109315
cograph::centrality(MCMLL_tna$Cognitive)
##           node degree_all strength_all closeness_all betweenness eigenvector
## 1     Argument          6    1.2489549      34.29463           1  0.97701354
## 2      Counter          5    0.1471751      37.64431           4  0.03008507
## 3 Disagreement          5    0.4141981      37.14602           0  0.02733112
## 4   Evaluation          6    1.2130269      37.64431           0  1.00000000
##    pagerank
## 1 0.4448065
## 2 0.0543097
## 3 0.0479051
## 4 0.4529787

9. Visualisation

cograph::splot() renders any netobject. Use it on the macro for the cluster-of-clusters view and on a within-cluster netobject for the fine-grained view.

cograph::splot(MCMLL_tna$macro,
               edge_labels = TRUE,
               edge_label_size = 0.7)

cograph::splot(MCMLL_tna$Cognitive,
               edge_labels = TRUE,
               edge_label_size = 0.7)

10. What to read off this analysis

  • Macro diagonal vs off-diagonal: how cluster-bound the discussion is. A high Reasoning -> Reasoning value means reasoning utterances are followed by more reasoning, not by social or procedural follow-ups.
  • Macro inits: which cluster opens sessions. If the discussion is Tutor-initiated, expect a high Tutor init.
  • Bootstrap CIs: a wide CI on a macro edge tells you the cluster-level flow is sensitive to which actors / sessions you sample. Narrow CIs with stars are the stable interactions worth interpreting.
  • Within-cluster networks: the conditional dynamics inside each cluster, useful for fine-grained pedagogical claims (e.g. Argument -> Counter-argument rates inside Cognitive).
  • Centralities: at the macro level, an outgoing-strength hub is a cluster the discussion frequently leaves; an incoming hub is a cluster it frequently lands in.
